label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is about determining which factor you cannot use to judge an Englishman's social class according to the passage. The options are (A) where he was born, (B) the way he speaks, (C) the clothes he wears, and (D) the interests he has. First, I need to refer back to the passage to find the relevant information. The passage says: "Most people know which class they belong to by the way they speak, their clothes, their interests or even the type of food they eat." So it lists the way of speaking, clothes, interests, and food as factors. That means (B), (C), and (D) are all factors that can be used to judge social class. Then there's a part that says, "For example, although many upper class people are rich and may own a lot of land, having a lot of money does not make a person upper class. It is also important to come from a particular kind of family, have friends who are considered suitable, have been to a certain type of private school and speak with the right kind of accent." Here, the passage is emphasizing that family background and education are important, but it doesn't mention birthplace as a determining factor. The passage says "come from a particular kind of family" but not "where he was born." So perhaps birthplace isn't a factor listed in the passage for judging social class. So the answer should be (A) where he was born. The passage doesn't say you can judge by birthplace, but you can judge by way of speaking, clothes, and interests. The fact
